UCD School of Public Health, Physiotherapy and Sports Science’s Post

UCD School of Public Health, Physiotherapy and Sports Science

788 followers

1mo
  • Report this post

Read below for a great new initiative on mental health and well-being capacity-building for public health professionals across Europe ⬇️ The programme involves several leading institutions and features participation from UCD SPHPSS Prof. Mary Codd, Dr. Jwenish K. and Dr Sonal Mohite. A call for candidates will open soon.

WHO Regional Office for Europe

86,910 followers

1mo Edited

👀 Are you a public health professional looking to build your skills in mental health and well‑being? Something’s definitely cooking... Earlier this month at UN City Copenhagen, a high‑level Expert Advisory Group met to help shape a new capacity‑building programme and a reference mental health curriculum for public health professionals across Europe. This work is being co‑developed with leading European public health, academic and mental health institutions, including EUPHA - European Public Health Association, The Association of Schools of Public Health in the European Region - ASPHER, University College Dublin (UCD), the National Institute of Public Health of Slovenia (NIJZ), Trimbos-instituut, and the European Psychiatric Association. This initiative is part of the European Commission‑funded project “Addressing mental health challenges in the EU, Iceland and Norway”, led by the WHO Regional Office for Europe, with a strong focus on mental health prevention and promotion, tackling stigma, and strengthening workforce resilience. What’s in the pipeline? ➡️ A series of accredited webinars, developed in collaboration with EUPHA. Join us during #EUPHW to learn more: https://bit.ly/4mMRROE ➡️ An in‑person conference later this year, following the webinars, creating space for deeper learning, exchange and collaboration. A call for candidates will open soon, with clear details on how and where to sign up. If mental health is part of your public health mission, this one’s for you! Stay tuned! -- Ledia Lazëri Charlotte Marchandise Emilia Aragón De León Cassie Redlich Ana Maria Tijerino Inestroza Laura Shields-Zeeman Natasha Azzopardi Muscat

    If you are a public health professional with a passion in mental health, working in the EU, Iceland and Norway, and interested on evidence‑informed approaches, addressing stigma, and integrating mental health in all policies, then this course is for you 👇

    WHO Regional Office for Europe

    86,910 followers

    1mo Edited

    📣 Calling all public health professionals working in the EU, Iceland and Norway! ❓ Are you working in public health and looking to strengthen your skills in mental health promotion and prevention? ❓ Do you want to learn how to apply evidence‑informed approaches, address stigma, and integrate mental health in all policies? Apply to be a part of our new accredited programme on strengthening public health workforce capacity for mental health promotion and prevention. The programme is implemented in collaboration with EUPHA - European Public Health Association and funded by the European Commission under the “Addressing mental health challenges in the European Union, Iceland and Norway” project.   What to expect: 👉 5 interactive webinars delivered between 10 September and 15 October 2026 (11:30–13:00 Central European Summer Time) 👉 One-day synthesis conference to be held on 10 November 2026 in Bilbao, Spain, as a pre-conference event of the EPH Conference. 🎓 Accreditation: Participants will be awarded 7.5 APHEA credits for completion of the webinar series, with up to 12.5 APHEA credits for participation in both the webinar series and the synthesis conference. ⏰ Apply by 26 May 2026 🔗 Find more information and link to submit your application here: https://lnkd.in/dyxFSgVJ Please note that selection will aim to ensure balanced representation, professional relevance, and meaningful inclusion of people with lived and living experience. Selected participants will be notified by 5 July 2026 Only a limited number of places are available. -- Ledia Lazëri Charlotte Marchandise Emilia Aragón De León Julian H.     🎥 Reflections from the Youth and Adolescent Mental Health session at the World Health Summit Regional Meeting 2026 A clear message from Naeem Dalal M.D and the panellists: transforming youth mental health in Africa requires shifting both funding and systems approaches. Aviwe Funani highlighted the need for sustainable financing, youth leadership, and shared an overview of the Being Initiative. Innocent Yusufu emphasised the need to move grassroots models from pilot projects into national systems through stronger partnerships and evidence generation. Amisa Rashid highlighted the importance of culturally appropriate solutions developed in consultation with young people. 📢 Watch the video for key reflections from the session. Being Initiative, Fondation Botnar, Orygen, Grand Challenges Canada, Africa Academy for Public Health (AAPH), and Nivishe Foundation

      We are delighted to see that the WHO and European Union are working together to develop a roadmap to scaling up digital mental health in Europe, taking into account the promotion and protection of child and adolescent mental health. The IMPROVA project involves the development of a school mental health programme supported by an online platform, with a view to sustainably integrating school mental health into education systems and policy frameworks across Europe and beyond 🧠

      WHO Regional Office for Europe

      86,910 followers

      2w

      👏 💡 A big step forward for digital mental health in Europe. Yesterday, WHO/Europe convened 42 experts from across the Region to co‑design the next chapter of digital mental health. Together with Ireland’s Health Service Executive and Department of Health, we are developing a new regional roadmap to help countries scale up digital solutions that are accessible, evidence‑based, and inclusive. The roadmap will focus on core action areas, including: 📌strong governance and accountability 📌robust digital infrastructure and standards 📌meaningful engagement and capacity building 📌continuous monitoring, evaluation, and learning This collaboration marks an exciting step toward transforming how mental health services are delivered and supported across the WHO European Region. The roadmap will align with key European priorities, including the Second European Programme of Work and the Regional Digital Health Action Plan 2023–2030. It also takes into account recent EU‑level developments, such as the Council of the European Union conclusions on promoting and protecting the mental health of children and adolescents in the digital era. This work is supported by the European Commission through the “Addressing mental health challenges in the EU, Iceland and Norway” project. Learn more and watch this space for updates: https://lnkd.in/eTngeH3g -- Ledia Lazëri Ana Maria Tijerino Inestroza Rachel Greenley David Novillo Ortiz, PhD #digitalhealth #mentalhealth

      This year, WHO/Europe will take part in 2026 European Public Health Week (#EUPHW), an annual initiative led by the European Public Health Association (#EUPHA), aimed at shining a spotlight on pressing issues and trends in public health. Every year, hundreds of organizations and thousands of people across Europe come together, as part of EUPHW, for a series of events, workshops, activities and dialogues. This year, WHO/Europe will host 2 special events: 👉 “What countries need to implement mental health in all policies: insights from European policy dialogues”, on 6 May from 10:00–11:30 CEST; and 👉 “MeND report: The mental health of Europe’s health workforce and what we must do about it”, on 8 May from 10:00–11:30 CEST. Both events are open to the public. For more information and registration please visit 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dVsh83Gt

      “Beyond the Publication: Taking Research Into Policy” 💻 📅 On Friday 24 April, our WHO Office on Quality of Care and Patient in Athens, was pleased to host the WHO Young Researchers Forum webinar, bringing together researchers and policy leaders for a dynamic discussion on how research can move beyond publication and translate into real-world policy and practice. 🔑 Key takeaways: • Turning research into actionable policy requires collaboration and clear communication • Strong examples showed how evidence can directly inform implementation • Arts and culture play a vital role in mental health and well-being • Cultural prescribing in Greece offers promising, innovative approaches  We were honored to welcome : • Dr Daniel Hayes – UCL & Social Prescribing Youth Network • Nicholas Yatromanolakis – Founder, Policy Lab | Former Deputy Minister of Contemporary Culture, Greece A big thank you to our speakers and everyone who joined and contributed to this engaging exchange. 🤝 #WHOGreece #AthensQoCOffice WHO Regional Office for Europe JOAO BREDA Dr Jennifer Hall Dr Anastasia Giannaki Penny Kalpaxi Eleni Maousidi-Zirganou Anastasia (Nansia) Gatopoulou Hannah Brunskill

    • Melissa T.
      1mo
      • Report this post

      A small reminder to myself this week about the value of reading widely, not just within paramedicine, but across different areas of research. I recently read “Gathering the Authentic Voices of Indigenous Young People” by Te Wai Barbarich-Unasa and colleagues (MAI Journal), which explores how to engage Indigenous young people meaningfully in research. The paper highlights that young people’s perspectives are essential in shaping better outcomes, and that engagement needs to support their voice, agency, and cultural identity, not just fit within adult-designed methods. A few takeaways I’m carrying into my own work: • Building relationships and trust takes time • Culturally grounded approaches are essential • Engagement spaces need to be approached carefully • Power dynamics are always present and need awareness • Participation should be genuine, not tokenistic Helpful reflections #Learning #PacificHealth #HealthEquity https://lnkd.in/eF5wc36B

      MAI Journal_Issue 15.1_Barbarish-Unasa-FNL.pdf journal.mai.ac.nz

      Taking Action at the Industry Forum 🗣️ For #MentalHealthAwareness week, we are putting a spotlight on a recent event co-hosted by the MHP and DATAMIND UK. Invited members of the Industry Alliance attended a Forum event centred on how Health and Wellbeing is incorporated into research and implemented in new technologies and social programmes around the UK to support people living with severe mental illness. Read the blog from our Cross-Hub ECR Fellow, Dr Justin Yang on his reflections from the day. 👉 https://lnkd.in/eBsMRrse Justin is a researcher bridging across Complex Emotions Hub, DATAMIND UK, and The Social Health Hub. At this event he was in conversation with academic peers, lived experience experts, industry members, and professionals from the public sector. "The point of collaboration is not to blur the differences between academia, industry, lived experience, policy, services, and the third sector. It is to create the conditions in which those differences can become something richer than parallel lines of work." If you are in industry and want to have the opportunity to attend events like these in the future, find out more and click here to join the Industry Alliance 🔗https://lnkd.in/eAqsqtJ5
